speaker: interviewer
text: The date is November 18, 1995. Survivor is Ruth Oppenheimer. Last name at birth is Hoflich. The interviewer is Louise Bobrow. The city is Phoenix in the state of Arizona. Country is United States of America. And the language is in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Louise Bobrow. Today's date is November 18, 1995. I'm conducting an interview with Ruth Oppenheimer. The interview's being conducted in Phoenix, Arizona. Good morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your complete name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ruth Oppenheimer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that for me, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: O-P-P-E-N-H-E-I-M-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ruth Hoflich.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: H-O-F-L-I-C-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: February 24, 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your present age?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Furth in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: F-U-R-T-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: What large city is Furth near?

speaker: interviewee
text: Furth had 5,000 inhabitants. And the next town was Nuremberg. I don't know how big Nuremberg was, but it was much larger.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the city that you grew up in was Furth--

speaker: interviewee
text: Furth.

speaker: interviewer
text: --correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Growing up, would you describe the city for me? What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very pleasant to live in Furth. We lived in a very nice beautiful apartment. And my first recollections were very pleasant in the family surroundings with a kosher household. And my father had his own business. He had a motorcycle business. He was one of the first to build motorcycles in Germany. And my first recollection, really bright recollection, with him was my sitting in front of him on the motorcycle and my sister behind him holding on. And he would take us for a ride. Unfortunately, this didn't last. The inflation in Germany decimated the money. And where at one time you could buy a whole motorcycle, the next day you could only buy a motor. And the following day, you couldn't buy anything. Because the money went into billions and trillions. And so it was very bad. At that time, my father-- my mother never worked. I don't believe she ever worked. Went in to selling automobiles for an automobile dealer. And we would go for rides in these touring cars because he always got one to demonstrate. So he would take us, and the cars then were not as good. So we would talk to the cars and tell them to go up the mountain and please don't slide down the mountain. And we would have very pleasant times again. But we always stayed in the same apartment. My parents rented that apartment when they wanted to get married in 1914. Unfortunately, the war broke out. And my father had to go into the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: So later on, with this automobile dealership he was able to get at dealership from Studebakers. And he would buy cars directly from Studebaker. They would be delivered in France at the port.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he and two other people would go and pick up the cars and drive them back to Nuremberg where he had a showroom. And they did that two or three times. And the cars, when they were delivered, had to be paid for. There was no such thing that the cars were owned by the company. You had to buy them. And then, you resold them. And this went on for many years. Life became sort of easy again until 1933. Of course, the political situation was very bad. And chancellors in Berlin would last from six to eight weeks. And then, the president of Germany would elect a new chancellor. And since the people couldn't find work, they followed the Nazi regime. And it was visible everywhere. In those days, I went to school first into first grade to fourth and then into the [GERMAN] for higher education. My English teacher, whose name was Glasser, very nice man, always told me, if you have to make a living speaking English, you're going to starve to death. Because I wasn't a very good English pupil. I've learned a few things in the meantime I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me go back a little bit, back up a little bit. Let me first ask you your parents' names. What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Julius, Julius Hoflich. And my mother was Clare Hoflich. And her maiden name was Benima.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I understand that you come from a family that was for a long time in Germany. Could you tell me how long, first of all, your family--

speaker: interviewee
text: My--

speaker: interviewer
text: --was in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: --grandfather on my mother's side came from the Dutch side. But his name, Benima is really Benimo, which means son of the lovely. And we understand that his family came through Spain to Holland. We don't have any documentation on that, but that's what we were always told. My father's side of the family lived in the Rhineland as I knew them. But before, we knew that as far back as 1793, they lived in the Rhineland. But there's also documentation in Nuremberg of a grave with our name on it. And this is near the Hans-Sachs burial ground. And we found it by chance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Both sets of grandparents. My mother's parents lived in Furth where we lived. My grandmother was a very short, sweet real Jewish grandmother who cooked and always had presents for us and was very sweet. And my grandfather was in business. He had an old iron and rags business and did very well. And because of that, my mother had a big dowry. And that's how my father built his motorcycle factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their names were Benima. His name was Asur. And her's was Caroline, Caroline. And my father's parents where Louise. And his name was Machen. And he owned a [NON-ENGLISH], butcher shop. And when he retired, the butcher shop went to the oldest son. My father-- in those days, you didn't send children to college-- went to learn a trade. And there was a third son who was killed in Verdun in the first World War. My grandmother never got over that. My uncle who took over the butcher shop married [NON-ENGLISH] was her name. And they ran the butcher shop. And they also had two daughters.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had two cousins. I have a sister named Liselott who lives in Israel. She's been there since 1936. Describe for me the home that you grew up in. What did it look like? What did the home look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The home was in the apartment on the third or fourth floor. And you had to walk up. There were no elevators. And you were responsible to clean the steps from one floor to the next. So we had a maid, and the maid had to do that. And we had a very large dining room with beautiful furniture and a living room, which is different than our living rooms here. Because in this living room, you had a desk and a piano and a sofa and a buffet with things that you needed because at that table, you would eat your dinner and your breakfast. And then, my parents had a bedroom. And my sister and I had a bedroom. But in this apartment, which was fairly large, there was only one bathroom. And in those days, that's all you knew. That's all you had, not like today. And the bathroom had a heater that had-- the water had to be heated with a wood stove. So it was rather primitive. And one day, the wood stove caught fire. And the firemen came. And then, my father put in gas water heater. So these things, I remember from way back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say then that you grew up in a rather well-to-do family situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Not wealthy, but well-to-do. We had more than we needed. I remember in the early days, we had two maids. But then later on, we only had one maid as we grew up. And as far as the grandparents, the one that lived in Furth, we saw all the time. But the ones that lived in the Rhineland, we would see-- I spent summer vacations there with my cousin. And there were two cousins, but one was older. So she wasn't interested in me. And it was very pleasant there too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language was spoken mainly in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other language?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We didn't speak any other language.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how some of the Jewish holidays were celebrated in your home, for example, Shabbos? Do you have any memories of Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Definitely. My mother [GERMAN] every Friday night. And the household was kosher. And but we did turn on lights. We were not strictly Orthodox. And on Friday nights, women did not go to shul. But on Saturday morning, we went. My father went to the shul that was not Orthodox. But my mother did. She went to the Mannheimer Shul in Furth. And there were five synagogues in one place, in a place. It's a [GERMAN]. And the reformed was in the center. And all the others were around the outside. And that was desecrated later on. On the high holidays, my father would wear a top hat. And he would be all dressed up. Everybody would be all dressed up. And we didn't drive. We walked to the synagogue. It was far away. And Passover Seder was spent together with friends in their home. And their grandparents would sit with white cushions behind them on the chair. And we would have to search for [GERMAN]. And it was a very pleasant being together. And of course, Passover, every time we walked home after, it's late at night, 12:00, 1 o'clock . There was the moon overhead. And you will notice Passover, there is always a full moon. And when we finally came to America, and we said, the moon followed us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other holidays that stand out in your mind that were celebrated, for example, Sukkot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We would go to-- we didn't have a Sukkot because we lived in an apartment. But other friends had. And we went there, and we helped decorate it. And Yom Kippur I would bring my mother a lemon-- because she was strictly fasting-- spiked with cloves for the smell. And she always said, I knew you were coming. I knew you were going to bring that to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a very active Jewish community in Furth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In the city of Furth-- I think I made a mistake before. There was 70,000 people in Furth, but there were 5,000 Jews. And they always called it little Jerusalem because of that, because there were so many Jews. There were Jewish butcher shops, kosher butcher shops. And lots of other people who had shops and stores. And there were many doctors, physicians. And as a matter of fact, Dr. Frank was the head of the main hospital there. He was-- there's later on, when we come to the Crystal Night, he was very much in the foreground then.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about Jewish cultural institutions?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all there. There were schools. There were schools for orphan children. And there was music hall. And there were lectures. And there were theater. And the little theater in Furth is well-known and is one of the most beautiful theaters in all of Germany. And it was-- the money to build it and finance it was mostly done by Jews. And this is still standing, and it was renovated. And the Baltimore Symphony went on a trip to Germany. And they played there. And it was said-- they said also it was the most beautiful hall that they had played in. I mean, they're big modern halls. But we did grow up with lots of culture. And I remember mother going to lectures. And there were opera-- very much so my father would play and sing with us on Sunday mornings. And we stood near the piano, and he would play. He played any instrument. He was a fantastic man.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe yourself as a youngster? What were you like growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a happy child. I was the second child. And I was spoiled I would think. I was very well-loved by everybody in there. I had dark hair. I still have a-- out of a bottle. And my sister was blonde. And she was the more outgoing. And I was the more quiet. But she would tell me stories. She was very nice. And we shared a room. And we went through the usual sicknesses, chicken pox and mumps, and stuff. But there came Dr. [NON-ENGLISH] with his real old-fashioned stethoscope made out of wood. It was a normal life. It was happy. And we would go on little trips on the weekend with our car. It was very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your dreams of the future when you were a little girl? Did you have ambitions of becoming something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my father always said, and he instilled it in us, I take my children to Hollywood, and we got it made. [LAUGHTER] He always claimed we were very-- pretty. And of course, we liked that. And I really was too young to have far flung ideas. But I wanted to become a dentist. And my aim was to become a dentist. But it wasn't possible. I wasn't allowed to go to university because I was Jewish. They wouldn't let Jews in. And the high school that I went to, I could have finished. But I didn't because my mother thought it was more important for me to learn something I could support myself with in case we do get to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you take up? What trade did you take up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sewing. I took up sewing. And I learned how to make suits and coats and dresses, really fine sewing, atelier sewing. But that was cut short too because the lady I worked for went to South Africa to Johannesburg. And it was impossible to find another job.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I did the second best thing. I became an assistant to a dentist. And I worked there for three years. And I liked that very much. He was very nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were two assistants. And we thought we had the most modern equipment in the world. And we did the best for all our patients. But when somebody came in that had a cold, he would stop everything after they were gone. And he would make us drink schnapps. And he says, I don't want you to catch this stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have many non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had some, yes. My parents had one couple that they were very friendly with. And the school I went to, of course, there were more Gentiles than Jews. And we lived right next door to Gentile people. And we were very friendly. It was all very pleasant. There was no distinction.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever experience-- before things got really bad, as you were a little girl-- did you ever experience any personal anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, never, never. The Jewish families in Furth were all well-liked and well-looked on. And it was a very good community. They were very charitable. They did everything for everybody, not just for Jews. And in return, I cannot remember any animosity before. I really can't. Much more later on, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: How close was Furth to Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Had one streetcar. They merged.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you go into Nuremberg often?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I became the dental assistant, I worked in Nuremberg. And I took the streetcar every day back and forth. It probably cost 10 [GERMAN]. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you spent quite a bit of time in Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Nuremberg, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a child too? Going with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the family, you went to the zoo. Nuremberg had a zoo. Furth did not have a zoo. And Nuremberg had this old castle, which was behind a wall. There were things going on behind the wall that my parents didn't talk about in front of the children. You can't talk in front of the children. It was just not done.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today of course, you talk about all that stuff very freely. But it was very interesting. And we would visit the old castle. It what they called a [GERMAN] with a chamber. What's it called? Torture chambers.

speaker: interviewee
text: That all was there. It was still there from the Middle Ages. But Nuremberg also was a very industrious town. And it also had an opera house. And it had a symphony. And they had the zoo where we went quite frequently. And then, the surrounding places was extremely pleasant. We would take-- drive out to the Franconian Switzerland, you know, Little Switzerland, which wasn't too far away. And we would picnic there. There was lots of culture, quartets and singing. And I remember my mother going to lectures and to-- they went to concerts when we were little.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did Hitler's rise to power begin to change your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, the first thing that it affected the family was that we had to get rid of the maid because she was Gentile. And this was in 1935. And that was in the household. Before then, it was different already. My father was still selling automobiles from France, Citroen, and from America, the Studebakers. Now, he had his own business by then. And one day, the Nazis came in and took five cars, drove them out of the showroom, and closed the door. The six of them came in. And five of them drove them out. And the sixth one just said goodbye. And the next day, they came again. They had counted how many cars were there. And they drove out all the rest of the cars. And my father says, now, wait a minute. You got to sign these papers because you have to pay me for that. And they said, if you ask us for payment once more, you're going to end up in the canal. Right across was a canal with water in it. And they had threatened him that they would drown him in there if he would ask for the money. So of course, he was bankrupt. The cars were paid for by him. Otherwise, he couldn't have gotten them. And he had an empty showroom. And he had employees there that he couldn't pay. And he was broke. And I think that was in 1934 or '35 already. By that time, he decided he had to make a living, and he was very handy. And the cars then did not come with ashtrays and things like that. And plastic was then coming on the market. Only it was called Bakelite. So he took forms. And he built ashtrays that could be hung into the doors and flower vases and this kind of accessories for the car. And he set up a little factory. And he went out, and he sold them. And he had a secretary who was also Gentile. And he had to get rid of her because as a Jew he was not allowed to work with a Gentile person. And by that time already, people around us were being arrested and shipped to Dachau and to other places. And we, of course, immediately, we were trying to say, let's go to America. And my father said, no. We're not going to America. This Hitler is not going to last just like the other chancellors never lasted. He's not going to last. And I'm not leaving this country. I fought here in the war. My brother died in the war. And we've been here for hundreds of years. We're not leaving. This is our country. But as time went, it became worse and worse. So when he had this little factory, one day somebody came in and said, Julius, you better watch out. They're coming. So he tried to sell the business. And he found somebody in Hamburg who had decided he wanted to buy the businesses. This was a good business. And he sold all the equipment. And he took the money and paid the bank because he had still owed money for the cars before. He said, no matter what I do, I am paying what I owe. I am an honorable man. And he packed up all of the equipment from the factory, put it in a truck. And he had promised this man he was coming with all the equipment to show him how to set it up and how to use it and how to start the business in Hamburg, which was quite a distance away. In the meantime, my mother and I still lived in the same apartment. But she took in a boarder who helped pay for the rent. And I went to work. And I made some money in the dentist's office. And between that, she and I were able to live in this apartment until-- I forget the date-- '38, 1938. Yeah, this was in 1938.
